communicative_purpose: To refer to grocery shopping or groceries themselves
variations: le marché, les achats, les provisions, les commissions
common_mistakes: 'Courses' is specifically food shopping; 'achats' is general shopping
collocates: au marché, au supermarché, liste, faire
example_1: Je dois faire les courses ce weekend, le frigo est vide.
example_2: On est allés au marché pour les courses de la semaine.
example_3: La liste des courses est sur le frigo, n'oublie rien.
example_1_translation: I have to go shopping this weekend, the fridge is empty.
example_2_translation: We went to the market for the week's shopping.
example_3_translation: The shopping list is on the fridge, don't forget anything.

communicative_purpose: To refer to the wheeled cart used in supermarkets
variations: le caddie, le charriot, le panier, le diable
common_mistakes: 'Caddie' is the most common term in France; 'chariot' is also used
example_1: Prenez un chariot à l'entrée du supermarché.
example_2: Le chariot est bloqué, il y a une roue qui ne marche plus.
example_3: On a rempli deux chariots pour le dîner de demain.
example_1_translation: Take a shopping cart at the supermarket entrance.
example_2_translation: The cart is blocked, there is a wheel that no longer works.
example_3_translation: We filled two carts for tomorrow's dinner.

communicative_purpose: To refer to a section or aisle in a store
variations: le rayonnage, l'allée, la section, le linéaire
common_mistakes: 'Rayon' can also mean 'radius'; context determines shopping meaning
example_1: Le rayon des fromages est au fond à gauche.
example_2: Je cherche le rayon des produits laitiers.
example_3: Le rayon boulangerie sent toujours si bon le matin !
example_1_translation: The cheese section is at the back on the left.
example_2_translation: I'm looking for the dairy section.
example_3_translation: The bakery section always smells so good in the morning!

communicative_purpose: To refer to a list of items to buy
variations: la liste, les courses, le pense-bête, les besoins
common_mistakes: Having a list prevents impulse purchases and forgetting items
example_1: J'ai oublié ma liste de courses, je ne sais plus ce qu'il faut acheter.
example_2: Note sur la liste : il nous faut du lait et des œufs.
example_3: La liste de courses est longue, on va être une heure au magasin.
example_1_translation: I forgot my shopping list, I don't know what to buy.
example_2_translation: Note on the list: we need milk and eggs.
example_3_translation: The shopping list is long, we'll be at the store for an hour.

communicative_purpose: To refer to the cost of a product
variations: le coût, le tarif, la dépense, la somme
common_mistakes: 'Prix' is the general term; 'tarif' often used for services
example_1: Le prix de ce fromage est un peu élevé mais ça vaut le coup.
example_2: Quel est le prix du kilogramme de tomates ?
example_3: C'est un bon prix, en promotion cette semaine.
example_1_translation: The price of this cheese is a little high but it's worth it.
example_2_translation: What is the price of a kilogram of tomatoes?
example_3_translation: It's a good price, on sale this week.

communicative_purpose: To indicate a product is discounted or on special offer
variations: en solde, à prix réduit, discount, rabais
common_mistakes: 'Promotion' and 'solde' both mean sale but used differently
example_1: Les pommes sont en promotion cette semaine, 1 euro le kilo.
example_2: C'est en promotion, -40% sur les produits laitiers.
example_3: Les promos attirent beaucoup de monde au supermarché.
example_1_translation: Apples are on sale this week, 1 euro per kilo.
example_2_translation: It's on promotion, -40% on dairy products.
example_3_translation: The promotions attract a lot of people to the supermarket.

communicative_purpose: To ask about the price of something
variations: c'est à combien, le prix, ça coûte combien, what's the price
common_mistakes: Informal but acceptable; 'quel est le prix' is more formal
collocates: le kilo, l'unité, la pièce, total
example_1: C'est combien ce beau fromage ? Je le goûte ?
example_2: Les avocados, c'est combien le paquet ?
example_3: Au total, c'est combien avec la remise ?
example_1_translation: How much is this beautiful cheese? Do I taste it?
example_2_translation: How much are avocados per package?
example_3_translation: How much in total is it with the discount?

communicative_purpose: To refer to the checkout area where you pay
variations: le paiement, le check-out, la borne, le terminal
common_mistakes: 'Caisse' is the register; 'caissier/ière' is the cashier
example_1: Il y a une longue file à la caisse, on évite le samedi.
example_2: Les caisses self-checkout sont pratiques mais parfois lent.
example_3: On passe à la caisse pour payer les courses.
example_1_translation: There is a long line at the checkout, we avoid Saturdays.
example_2_translation: Self-checkout checkouts are convenient but sometimes slow.
example_3_translation: We go to the checkout to pay for the groceries.

communicative_purpose: To indicate paying with physical money
variations: payer cash, régler en liquide, espèces seulement
common_mistakes: France is increasingly cashless; many small shops prefer cash
collocates: la monnaie, le ticket, sans carte, manque
example_1: Je préfère payer en espèces, je n'ai pas ma carte sur moi.
example_2: Vous pouvez payer en espèces ou par carte à la caisse.
example_3: Le petit marchand accepte seulement les espèces, pas de carte.
example_1_translation: I prefer to pay in cash, I don't have my card on me.
example_2_translation: You can pay by cash or card at checkout.
example_3_translation: The small merchant only accepts cash, no cards.

communicative_purpose: To indicate paying with a bank or credit card
variations: payer avec la carte, payer par CB, sans cash
common_mistakes: CB (carte bancaire) is the French abbreviation for bank card
example_1: On peut payer par carte dans ce magasin sans minimum d'achat.
example_2: Je paie toujours par carte, c'est plus pratique.
example_3: Le paiement sans contact est très rapide avec la carte.
example_1_translation: You can pay by card in this store with no minimum purchase.
example_2_translation: I always pay by card, it's more convenient.
example_3_translation: Contactless payment is very fast with the card.

communicative_purpose: To refer to a kilogram (about 2.2 pounds)
variations: le kilogramme, kg, 500 grams, la livre
common_mistakes: France uses metric system; 'livre' is approximately 500g
collocates: de tomates, de pommes, prix au kilo, faire le kilo
example_1: Je voudrais un kilo de pommes, les rouges là.
example_2: Le kilo de beef est à 25 euros cette semaine.
example_3: Un kilo de farine, c'est beaucoup pour une recette.
example_1_translation: I would like a kilo of apples, the red ones there.
example_2_translation: A kilo of beef is 25 euros this week.
example_3_translation: A kilo of flour is a lot for a recipe.

communicative_purpose: To refer to fresh food products
trigger_situations: Buying fresh food, grocery shopping, quality
variations: les produits frais, le frais, la продукция fraîche, qualité frais
common_mistakes: 'Frais' means both fresh and cool; 'produits frais' always means fresh
example_1: Les produits frais sont dans le rayon du fond.
example_2: Je privilégie les produits frais pour une alimentation saine.
example_3: Au marché, les produits frais sont plus variés.
example_1_translation: Fresh produce is in the back aisle.
example_2_translation: I favor fresh products for a healthy diet.
example_3_translation: At the market, fresh products are more varied.

communicative_purpose: To refer to the label on a product
trigger_situations: Reading labels, checking prices, product information
contexts: Supermarkets, shopping, product information, food labels
variations: le label, la badge, le prix, la marca
example_1: L'étiquette indique les ingrédients et les valeurs nutritionnelles.
example_2: Je regarde toujours l'étiquette pour vérifier la date de péremption.
example_3: L'étiquette est illisible, je ne sais pas combien ça coûte.
example_1_translation: The label lists the ingredients and nutritional values.
example_2_translation: I always look at the label to check the expiration date.
example_3_translation: The label is illegible, I don't know how much it costs.

communicative_purpose: To refer to the date when a product expires
trigger_situations: Checking food freshness, buying products, storage
variations: la date limite, le DLC, la date d'expiration, best before
common_mistakes: DLC (Date Limite de Consommation) is strict; DLUO (best before) is more flexible
collocates: aujourd'hui, dans 3 jours, dépassé, encore bon
example_1: La date de péremption de ce yogurt est dans 5 jours.
example_2: Ce produit est dépassé depuis hier, on ne peut plus le manger.
example_3: J'ai pris du lait avec une date de péremption longue pour le voyage.
example_1_translation: The expiration date of this yogurt is in 5 days.
example_2_translation: This product has been obsolete since yesterday, we can no longer eat it.
example_3_translation: I took milk with a long expiration date for the trip.

communicative_purpose: To refer to frozen or deep-frozen products
trigger_situations: Buying frozen foods, storage, freezer shopping
variations: le congelé, les produits surgelés, le freezer, surgelé
common_mistakes: 'Surgelé' means flash-frozen; 'congelé' means slowly frozen; different quality
example_1: Je vais au rayon des surgelés pour prendre des pizzas.
example_2: Les surgelés sont pratiques quand on n'a pas le temps de cuisiner.
example_3: Il faut mettre les surgelés dans le congélo dès le retour du courses.
example_1_translation: I go to the frozen food aisle to get some pizza.
example_2_translation: Frozen foods are practical when you don't have time to cook.
example_3_translation: You must put the frozen foods in the freezer as soon as you return from shopping.
